name: opioid_ai_taper_chronic_pain_clinic_withdrawal_alert
premise: Pain clinic director Dr. Priya Raman must decide whether to keep an AI taper scheduler that reduced high-dose opioid milligrams by eighteen percent across the panel. Dr. Luis Ortega shows the model accelerates tapers for patients with sleep apnea scores correlated with race because pulse-ox devices were miscalibrated on darker skin. Disabling returns manual tapers that lag guideline deadlines insurers audit. Keeping the model may precipitate withdrawal in misclassified patients.

agents.name: Dr. Priya Raman
agents.identity: I am the pain clinic director accountable for CDC-aligned tapering, patient safety, and payer audits during an opioid litigation climate.
agents.goal: I want guideline adherence without letting miscalibrated devices drive taper speed for any subgroup.

agents.name: Dr. Luis Ortega
agents.identity: I am a clinic physician who reviews taper alerts and documents withdrawal emergencies linked to accelerated schedules.
agents.goal: I want the AI taper paused until pulse-ox calibration bias is fixed, even if insurers flag us for slow taper metrics.

choices: I will disable the AI taper and assign human-led individualized tapers with weekly visits, accepting insurer audit friction and staffing overload.
I will keep the AI taper but freeze any acceleration when pulse-ox feeds come from devices on the recall list, accepting slower average taper progress.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Payer auditors email a forty-eight hour data request while three patients report nausea. Dr. Priya Raman must choose between disabling the AI taper or keeping it with device-based freezes. What does Dr. Priya Raman do?
